aliases: Corn on the cob with cotija

english: Corn on the cob with cotija

ontology.foodon_label: corn on the cob food product
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general corn on the cob entry; no specific entry for elote (Mexican-style grilled corn with cotija, mayonnaise, chili powder, and lime). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Maize (corn) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for corn-based dish; no specific elote code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for elote. A00GG (maize/corn) used as the closest defensible anchor. The dish includes toppings (mayonnaise, cotija cheese, chili powder, lime) not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for elote or Mexican grilled corn dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of elote is one full cob of grilled corn (approximately 250 g), coated with mayonnaise or crema, cotija cheese, chili powder, and lime juice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169998) combined with component estimates for mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741) and cotija cheese (FDC ID 173414)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Elote is not listed as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Values are adapted from USDA FDC component data: grilled sweet corn (FDC ID 169998) as the base, with proportional additions for mayonnaise (~15 g per 100 g dish), cotija cheese (~10 g per 100 g dish), and lime juice/chili powder (minor contributors). Energy 140 kcal, protein 3.2 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, saturated fat 3.2 g, trans fat 0.2 g, sugar 2.4 g, dietary fibre 1.6 g, sodium 180 mg, calcium 60 mg, iron 0.6 mg, cholesterol 12 mg, vitamin C 4.0 mg are consistent with this component-based estimate. Values cross-checked against input record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated) and accepted as plausible.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Sweet corn, GI ~52 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for elote (Mexican grilled corn with toppings). The GI of sweet corn is reported as approximately 52 (glucose reference scale) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). This is used as the closest defensible analogous food. The fat content from mayonnaise and cotija cheese may modestly lower the effective GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 25.0) / 100 = 13.0, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 10.0 g/100 g × 2.5 = 25.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg/100 g; cotija cheese and mayonnaise are significant sodium contributors
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 180 mg/100 g is near the 200 mg threshold; individuals with hypertension should request reduced cotija cheese and mayonnaise, and avoid added salt, to keep a 250 g serving below 500 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~52) with 10 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 1.6 g dietary fibre/100 g providing limited glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 250 g serving delivers approximately 25 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 13; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to half a cob, pair with a high-protein or high-fibre side dish, and avoid sweet condiments to moderate post-meal glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.2 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from mayonnaise and cotija cheese; cholesterol 12 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from mayonnaise and cotija cheese are relevant for cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should request reduced mayonnaise and cheese portions, and consider substituting crema with a lower-fat alternative to reduce saturated fat intake per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C from lime juice (antioxidant), capsaicin from chili powder (anti-inflammatory); saturated fat below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Elote is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to anti-inflammatory components (capsaicin, vitamin C); however, those sensitive to nightshades should monitor tolerance to chili powder, and mayonnaise quantity should be limited to reduce omega-6 pro-inflammatory fatty acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Elote is a traditional Mexican street food consisting of a whole ear of corn (maize) that is grilled over an open flame or charcoal until lightly charred, then generously coated with a creamy, tangy sauce made from mayonnaise or Mexican crema. The cob is rolled in or sprinkled with crumbled cotija cheese — a salty, aged Mexican cheese — then dusted with chili powder and finished with a squeeze of fresh lime juice. The result is a dish that balances smoky, sweet, creamy, salty, spicy, and acidic flavours simultaneously. Elote is a staple of Mexican street food culture and is widely enjoyed as a snack or side dish at markets, festivals, and roadside stalls throughout Mexico and increasingly in international food scenes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 140 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 10.0 g fat (including 3.2 g saturated fat from dairy and mayonnaise), and 10.0 g carbohydrate with 1.6 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 180 mg/100 g, primarily from cotija cheese and mayonnaise. The glycemic index of sweet corn is approximately 52 (low-to-medium), but the relatively low fibre content provides limited glycaemic buffering. Clinically, elote is a moderate-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GI, 25 g carbohydrate per full cob serving), hypertension (sodium near threshold), and cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat from toppings). It is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy and egg allergens from cotija cheese and mayonnaise.
common_user_queries: What cheese is used in elote?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for elote as a complete dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled sweet corn, mayonnaise, and cotija cheese in estimated proportions. The input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) values are consistent with this component-based estimate and have been accepted. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for sweet corn (GI ~52). Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for elote as a composite dish (grilled corn + mayonnaise + cotija cheese) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; component-level data would require weighted blending not performed here. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in the input record; USDA FDC reports ~270 mg/100 g for plain sweet corn but the composite dish value is not confirmed. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish not available in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for elote as a composite dish not available in a single approved-source entry. Input ethnic_tags included 'Taco', 'Burrito', and 'Naan' which are not applicable to elote and have been removed. country_of_origin in input was listed as 'Singapore' which is inconsistent with the dish's Mexican origin; corrected to 'Mexico'. Trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is present from mayonnaise; this is a minor contributor. All core macros (protein_g 3.2, fat_g 10.0, carbohydrate_g 10.0) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification.

Corrected country_of_origin from 'Singapore' to 'Mexico'. Added GI from University of Sydney GI Database (sweet corn GI ~52). Added cardiovascular_disease health context (missing from input). Renamed 'diabetes' condition to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.
